title: quadlet->systemd generator uses wrong cidfile for non-root users

comments.body:
Hi,

using Ubuntu server 24.04.3 and podman 4.9.3, I found that problem:

podman recommends to prefer rootless over rootful mode for security. 


Therefore, I created a service user named containers with a uid < 1000, and tried to run a container as this user, configuring with quadlets. 


Problem 1: In contrast to regular users, systemd does not start an systemd user instance for system accounts, therefore it is not possible to put the quadlet file in the users home directory like with regular users. 

To cope with that, I've put the quadlet like a root users's one into /etc/containers/systemd and put something like


[Container]
ContainerName=...
Image=... 
PublishPort=...
AutoUpdate=local
Pull=newer
[Service]
User=containers
Environment=XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/997
[Install]
WantedBy=default.target


in the quadlet file. 

Doesn't start:

Error: creating idfile: open /run/gemstash.cid: permission denied


because podman's quadlet generator insists on using 
 --cidfile=%t/%N.cid

where %t is /run for root and XDG_RUNTIME_DIR for non-root-users, but it can't be overridden with Environment=XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/997

The quadlet generator needs an option to override %t or %t/%N.cid

> [Service]
User=containers




The systemd directive `User=` is not supported in quadlets.



The GitHub PR [26552](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26552) introduced a warning that
will be logged if `podman-system-generator` encounters `User=`, `Group=` or `DynamicUser=`
under the `[Service]` section.

For example
```
Warning: using key User in the Service group is not supported - use at your own risk
```

References:
 * https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/af91cae1c5bb42ae7b2f383eb5525e34d6e14789/cmd/quadlet/main.go#L369
* https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/20573


__side note:__


> [Service]
User=containers

The username you chose `containers` has a special meaning in podman. 

For details, see
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/af91cae1c5bb42ae7b2f383eb5525e34d6e14789/troubleshooting.md?plain=1#L1485

I recommend using another username.

--------------------------------------


> Problem 1: In contrast to regular users, systemd does not start an systemd user instance for system accounts, therefore it is not possible to put the quadlet file in the users home directory like with regular users.

Systemd starts a user instance if lingering has been enabled.

```
sudo loginctl enable-linger myuser
```

To creata system user you could probably run

```
sudo useradd --add-subids-for-system --system --create-home myuser
```

or

```
sudo useradd --add-subids-for-system --system --create-home --shell /sbin/nologin myuser
```

> The systemd directive User= is not supported in quadlets.


So what is the proposed and intended way to start a rootless container from a system user then?

Not using quadlets at all?

You could use this path for the container unit:
```
/home/myuser/.config/containers/systemd/demo.container 
```

Here is an example that I ran on Fedora 43 that has podman 5.6.2

1. Create system user `myuser`
   ```
   sudo useradd \
          --add-subids-for-system \
          --create-home \
          --shell /sbin/nologin \
          --system \
	  myuser
   ```
2. Create directory
   ```
   sudo -u myuser mkdir -p /home/myuser/.config/containers/systemd
   ```
3. Create container unit file
   ```
   sudo -u myuser sh -c 'echo -e "[Container]\nImage=alpine\nExec=sleep inf\n" > /home/myuser/.config/containers/systemd/demo.container'
   ```
4. Show file
   ```
   sudo cat /home/myuser/.config/containers/systemd/demo.container
   ```
   The following output is printed
   ```
   [Container]
   Image=alpine
   Exec=sleep inf
   ```
5. Enable linger
   ```
   sudo loginctl enable-linger myuser
   ```
6. Reload the user systemd user manager for the user __myuser__
   ```
   sudo systemctl --machine myuser@ --user daemon-reload
   ```
7. Start the service
   ```
   sudo systemctl --machine myuser@ --user start demo.service
   ```
8. Verify that the service is running
   ```
   sudo systemctl --machine myuser@ --user is-active demo.service
   ```
   The following output is printed
   ```
   active
   ```

@eriksjolund : Thank you. 

This is almost exactly what I did. With one detail different: 

I had tried to systemctl --user daemon-reload (and other commands) as the system user and always got the error message 
Failed to connect to bus: No medium found
because even after enable-linger, the system didn't start a systemd on dbus. 

Your command is different:  sudo systemctl --machine myuser@ --user daemon-reload

That function of --machine was not obvious from the man page of systemctl. 


That detail of systemctl was the missing link. Since that hadn't worked, I had assumed that linger wouldn't work for system users, and that configuring and enabling service requires a regular login through PAM. 


What about putting a section about this in the quadlets man page?

This seems to be a dup of https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26671

@hadmut Sure please open a PR to add this to the docs.

> Here is an example that I ran on Fedora 43 that has podman 5.6.2

I added the longer example (somewhat modified) in a new section:
[_Controlling quadlets of another user account_](https://github.com/eriksjolund/podman-quadlet-docs#controlling-quadlets-of-another-user-account)
in
https://github.com/eriksjolund/podman-quadlet-docs
